title: When Role-playing, Do Models Believe What They Say?
abstract: arXiv:2606.11502v3 Announce Type: replace-cross  Abstract: Language models can state that "the Earth orbits the Sun" and, when role-playing Aristotle, assert the opposite. Recent work argues that persona adoption is fundamental to how language models behave, with models selecting the most appropriate persona for a given context. Does such role-playing merely change the model's outputs, or does it also affect what the model internally represents as truthful? We study this question using the role-play of characters whose beliefs differ from the modern consensus, and induce personas with a number of different methods: prompting, in-context learning (ICL), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and Open Character Training (OCT), and Emergent Misalignment (EM). We measure belief internalization across these approaches with truth probes and with behavioral tests, finding a broad spectrum of belief internalization. Prompting, ICL, and SFT change what the model says with little representational change.

Title: When Role-playing, Do Models Believe What They Say?
Abstract: arXiv:2606.11502v3 Announce Type: replace-cross  Abstract: Language models can state that "the Earth orbits the Sun" and, when role-playing Aristotle, assert the opposite. Recent work argues that persona adoption is fundamental to how language models behave, with models selecting the most appropriate persona for a given context. Does such role-playing merely change the model's outputs, or does it also affect what the model internally represents as truthful? We study this question using the role-play of characters whose beliefs differ from the modern consensus, and induce personas with a number of different methods: prompting, in-context learning (ICL), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and Open Character Training (OCT), and Emergent Misalignment (EM). We measure belief internalization across these approaches with truth probes and with behavioral tests, finding a broad spectrum of belief internalization. Prompting, ICL, and SFT change what the model says with little representational change.
translated_title: 当进行角色扮演时，模型会相信它们所说的话吗？
translated_abstract: 语言模型可以陈述“地球围绕太阳转”，而在扮演亚里士多德时，则会断言相反的观点。近期研究认为，角色采纳是语言模型行为的基础，模型会根据给定上下文选择最合适的角色。这种角色扮演是否仅仅改变了模型的输出，还是也影响了模型内部对真实性的表征？我们通过扮演其信念与现代共识相悖的角色来研究这一问题，并采用多种方法诱导角色：提示、上下文学习（ICL）、监督微调（SFT）、开放角色训练（OCT）以及突发性错位（EM）。我们通过真相探针和行为测试来衡量这些方法中的信念内化程度，发现信念内化存在广泛差异。提示、ICL和SFT主要改变模型的输出，而表征变化很小。
tldr: 研究发现，不同的角色扮演诱导方法（如提示、上下文学习、监督微调）主要改变语言模型的输出，但对其内部信念表征的影响较小，即模型并未真正内化角色的信念。
en_tdlr: The study finds that different role-playing induction methods (such as prompting, in-context learning, and supervised fine-tuning) primarily change the output of language models, but have limited impact on their internal belief representations, meaning the models do not truly internalize the character's beliefs.
